title: Latin America’s Slow But Steady Pivot Toward Africa
gist: Latin America/Caribbean and Africa are slowly building a South-South partnership — a March 2026 first-ever CELAC-Africa ministerial, a direct Tema-Cartagena shipping route, and rising trade — framed as a tool of multipolar power. The bloc commands ~half the UN General Assembly and could reshape rules on trade, finance and critical minerals, but past summits failed to sustain momentum and institutionalization is the missing piece.

insights.claim: Latin America/Caribbean and Africa together hold 87 UN votes — almost half the General Assembly — giving the combined bloc leverage to set high-level agendas and contest global economic rules.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: A near-majority UN voting bloc is a structural lever to watch for shifts in multilateral trade, finance, and climate negotiations.
insights.quote: The regions hold a combined 87 votes, almost half the UN General Assembly
insights.locator: South-South coalitions are not just expressions of solidarity

insights.claim: Both regions are trapped by the same international financial architecture — trade regimes penalizing industrial strategy, expensive and conditional financing, and technology rules designed to preserve rather than transfer capabilities.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: Shared grievance over financial-architecture conditionality is the glue for coordinated pressure on multilateral development banks and liquidity mechanisms.
insights.quote: They are trapped by the same international financial architecture
insights.locator: Despite their importance, both regions remain sidelined

insights.claim: What is emerging is a form of multipolar autonomy, with Global South nations broadening ties to multiple partners and growing more resilient amid fragmentation of the international order.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: Reinforces the multipolar/diversification macro theme: emerging blocs hedging away from traditional Western-centric trade and finance.
insights.quote: What is emerging is not only friendly bilateral relations but also a form of multipolar autonomy.
insights.locator: What is emerging is not only friendly bilateral relations

insights.claim: The two regions together hold over two billion people, more than a third of the world's freshwater, the two largest rainforests, and significant reserves of critical minerals for the energy transition.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: Combined control of critical-mineral and water resources gives the bloc latent supply-chain leverage in the energy transition.
insights.quote: significant reserves of the critical minerals on which the global energy transition depends
insights.locator: Together, the regions have more than two billion people

insights.claim: Latin America/Caribbean exports to Africa have grown more than 60% over the last decade — mainly agricultural products and consumer goods — yet CELAC's exports to Africa are still just 0.3% of global trade.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: Tiny base plus fast growth flags long-runway upside in LatAm agri-exports to a food-importing Africa.
insights.quote: CELAC’s exports to Africa make up just 0.3% of global trade

insights.claim: Africa is a net food importer whose population is forecast to grow to 2.5 billion by 2050, positioning Latin America/Caribbean as a key agricultural supplier.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: Structural African food-demand growth supports a multi-decade thesis for LatAm agribusiness exposure.
insights.quote: a net food importer whose population is forecasted to grow to 2.5 billion by 2050
insights.locator: The Development Bank of Latin America and the Caribbean, CAF

insights.claim: Following the March 2026 CELAC-Africa forum, Ghana and Colombia agreed to establish a direct shipping route between Tema and Cartagena, bypassing European hubs that added cost and time to transatlantic South-South trade.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: Direct South-South shipping lanes cut logistics cost and are a concrete, trackable signal that the pivot is moving from rhetoric to trade infrastructure.
insights.quote: a direct shipping route between the ports of Tema and Cartagena, bypassing the European hubs

insights.claim: The March 2026 Bogotá meeting was the first-ever ministerial-level CELAC-Africa meeting, and under Uruguay (CELAC) and Burundi (AU) presidencies the blocs could hold a first Heads of State CELAC-Africa Summit in Addis Ababa next year.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: A first heads-of-state summit would be the milestone confirming the partnership has escalated beyond technical cooperation.
insights.quote: the first-ever ministerial-level meeting between the Community of Latin American and Caribbean States (CELAC) and Africa

insights.claim: Prior Africa-South America summits in 2006, 2009 and 2013 failed to sustain the momentum needed for a robust partnership, making institutionalization rather than political will the key variable this time.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: History of failed momentum is the base-rate caution: discount the pivot until cooperation is embedded in CELAC's institutional framework.
insights.quote: neither was able to sustain the momentum needed to build a robust partnership
insights.locator: The push for deeper cooperation is not new

insights.claim: The authors argue the bi-regional agenda must be institutionalized into CELAC's framework with regular summits rather than relying on individual administrations' political will, and that cooperation should transcend political alignments.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: Durability depends on de-coupling the agenda from leftist-government leadership cycles — watch for institutional anchoring as the durability test.
insights.quote: rather than relying on the political will of individual administrations
insights.locator: For the regions to amplify their voices

insights.claim: Coordinated bi-regional positions could resist multilateral trade provisions that constrain industrial-policy tools (subsidies, local content, strategic procurement) and push for cheaper development finance and reformed risk pricing by multilateral banks.
insights.speaker: Andrea Ordóñez and Alejandra Meneses
insights.so_what: If realized, joint advocacy on industrial-policy space and DFI risk pricing would shift terms for EM development finance.
insights.quote: Coordinated positions in multilateral trade negotiations could resist provisions that constrain industrial policy tools
insights.locator: Coordinated positions in multilateral trade negotiations
